## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Walk me through L4 versus L7 — when do you use each?"**
**The mechanism:** L4 routes by IP+port without reading the request — fast, cheap, protocol-agnostic, but can't route on URL or cookie. L7 terminates the connection and reads the HTTP, enabling path-based routing, stickiness, TLS termination, and retries, at higher CPU cost. We use a two-tier design: L4 fans connections to the L7 tier.
**🗣️ In plain words:** L4 is a fast bouncer who only checks where you're going; L7 is a concierge who reads your request and decides exactly which room.
**💬 One-liner:** "L4 for cheap fast fan-out, L7 for anything that needs to read the request — I use both, L4 in front of L7."

**❓ "A backend dies mid-request — what does the user see?"**
**The mechanism:** for the connection already on the dead backend, it drops; for an idempotent request the L7 tier retries on another healthy backend (retry budget bounded). Health checks eject the dead backend within ~6s so no new requests go there.
**🗣️ In plain words:** the one request in flight retries on a healthy server; everyone else just avoids the dead one within a few seconds.
**💬 One-liner:** "In-flight idempotent requests retry elsewhere; the dead backend is ejected in about six seconds so nobody else hits it."

**❓ "How fast do you detect a dead backend, and why not faster?"**
**The mechanism:** 3 consecutive failed probes at a 2s interval = ~6s. Faster (shorter interval or fewer failures) increases false ejections from transient blips; we balance with EWMA smoothing and a quorum so we don't flap.
**🗣️ In plain words:** about six seconds — we could go faster but then a hiccup would wrongly kick out healthy servers.
**💬 One-liner:** "Roughly six seconds — fast enough to matter, slow enough not to flap on a blip."

**❓ "The load balancer is the single point of failure — how do you fix that?"**
**The mechanism:** the VIP is a floating address; an active-passive pair across two AZs runs sub-second VRRP heartbeats (~250ms), and the standby claims the VIP via gratuitous ARP in ~1.5s if the master dies, inside our ~2s budget. A lease in etcd fences against split-brain.
**🗣️ In plain words:** the front-door address can jump to a backup machine in under two seconds if the main one dies, so the door never closes.
**💬 One-liner:** "The VIP floats — a standby grabs it in under two seconds, fenced by an etcd lease so we never split-brain."

**❓ "What's split-brain and how do you prevent it?"**
**The mechanism:** if the heartbeat link fails but both LB nodes are alive, both might claim the VIP, corrupting forwarding. We require winning an etcd lease before claiming the VIP; the loser self-demotes, so only one node ever owns it.
**🗣️ In plain words:** two machines both thinking they're in charge — we prevent it by making them grab a single shared token first; only one can hold it.
**💬 One-liner:** "Split-brain is dual masters; I fence it with an etcd lease so only the lease-holder claims the VIP."

**❓ "How do you do a flash-sale / traffic-spike — does the LB cope?"**
**The mechanism:** at our scale one node handles 40x our peak, so the LB itself is never the constraint — it's the backends. The LB applies back-pressure (bounded queues, 503/429 + Retry-After) so a backend overload doesn't crash the LB, and slow-start ramps new backends as autoscaling adds them.
**🗣️ In plain words:** the load balancer has tons of headroom; if the servers behind it can't keep up, it pushes back politely instead of collapsing.
**💬 One-liner:** "The LB has 40x headroom; under a real spike it sheds load with 429s rather than queue without limit."

**❓ "Can the same request be sent to two backends and processed twice?"**
**The mechanism:** only on a retry — if the LB retries a request it thinks failed but the original actually succeeded. We only auto-retry *idempotent* methods (GET, or POST with an idempotency key); non-idempotent requests without a key are not retried.
**🗣️ In plain words:** we only retry requests that are safe to repeat; anything that changes money or state needs a key so a repeat is harmless.
**💬 One-liner:** "We only retry idempotent requests; for the rest the client supplies an idempotency key so a retry is a no-op."

**❓ "How do sticky sessions survive backends being added or removed?"**
**The mechanism:** stickiness uses consistent hashing — a ring where each session key maps to a backend, so adding/removing one backend only remaps that backend's slice, not everyone. Bounded-load caps prevent one backend from being overwhelmed by hot keys.
**🗣️ In plain words:** we use a hash ring so adding a server only reshuffles a small slice of users, not all of them.
**💬 One-liner:** "Consistent hashing — add or drop a backend and only its slice of sticky sessions moves, not the whole map."

**❓ "Least-connections across multiple LB nodes needs shared state — how?"**
**The mechanism:** we avoid the shared state. Power-of-two-choices samples two backends per request and routes to the less loaded — provably near-optimal with zero coordination, so no global counter on the hot path.
**🗣️ In plain words:** instead of polling every server, we peek at two and pick the less busy one — almost as good, no central tally needed.
**💬 One-liner:** "I don't share global counts — power-of-two-choices gets near-optimal balancing with no coordinator."

**❓ "What happens during a deploy — do users get errors?"**
**The mechanism:** connection draining — the backend goes to DRAINING (no new connections, finish existing ones) with a bounded drain timeout, then is removed. New backends ramp via slow-start. Users see no errors unless a request exceeds the drain timeout, which then returns a clean close to retry.
**🗣️ In plain words:** we stop sending new traffic to a server being retired but let current requests finish, so deploys are invisible.
**💬 One-liner:** "Draining — stop new connections, finish in-flight ones up to a timeout; deploys are invisible to users."

**❓ "What if the health checker itself goes down or is wrong?"**
**The mechanism:** each LB node also runs its own checks (defense in depth), and ejection requires a quorum across nodes plus passive confirmation from real traffic — so no single checker's failure or false verdict is fatal. A min-healthy floor prevents black-holing.
**🗣️ In plain words:** no single watcher can take down the fleet — several have to agree, and we never kick out so many that nothing's left.
**💬 One-liner:** "Checks are quorum'd and corroborated by real traffic, with a min-healthy floor — one bad checker can't black-hole the fleet."

**❓ "How do you stop a client from forging which backend they hit, or tampering with config?"**
**The mechanism:** clients can't address backends directly — they only see the VIP; backend selection is server-side. Control-plane calls require a signed JWT/mTLS identity with object-level authz (the operator must own the pool they edit); health/state fields are server-set and read-only.
**🗣️ In plain words:** clients only know the front door, never the individual servers, and operators can only touch pools they own.
**💬 One-liner:** "Clients see only the VIP; config edits need a signed identity and an ownership check on that specific pool."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut cost?"**
**The mechanism:** egress bandwidth (~$450/mo at 9 TB) is the biggest variable cost; LB compute (~$1,500/mo) is mostly fixed HA overhead. The biggest lever is reducing the HA node count — which we won't, because no-SPOF is the requirement; instead per-request cost falls naturally as volume grows on the same footprint.
**🗣️ In plain words:** sending bytes back to users costs the most variable money; the boxes are mostly the price of redundancy.
**💬 One-liner:** "Egress is the variable bill; compute is fixed availability overhead — at higher volume the per-request cost drops on the same boxes."

**❓ "How would you extend this to multiple regions?"**
**The mechanism:** layer anycast — announce the same VIP from each region so the network routes users to the nearest, with DNS-based health failover between regions. Each region keeps its own active-passive VIP internally. Config (etcd) either federates per-region or replicates with a single write region.
**🗣️ In plain words:** announce the same address from every region so people hit the closest one, and route around a dead region automatically.
**💬 One-liner:** "Anycast the VIP per region with DNS health failover between them — the regional active-passive story stays as-is inside each."

**❓ "How would you support gRPC / long-lived streaming connections?"**
**The mechanism:** long-lived connections make least-connections (or least-request for HTTP/2 multiplexing) essential and round-robin useless; draining must wait for streams to end (with a longer timeout), and the LB needs HTTP/2-aware L7 (Envoy handles this natively). Connection rate drops but concurrency rises, so conn-table sizing matters more.
**🗣️ In plain words:** for long connections you balance by how busy each server is, not by turns, and you wait longer to drain.
**💬 One-liner:** "Switch to least-request, lengthen drain timeouts, and use HTTP/2-aware L7 — Envoy already does this."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "A load balancer is the single front door that spreads every incoming request across a fleet of identical servers and routes around dead ones. The design splits into two halves. The data-plane half is a two-tier forwarder — a dumb-fast L4 layer fans connections to a smart L7 layer that reads the HTTP and picks a backend by round-robin, least-connections via power-of-two-choices, or consistent-hash for stickiness — all reading one shared health map. The control-plane half keeps that map honest and keeps the front door alive: it actively and passively health-checks backends, ejects the dead within about six seconds without flapping, and — the hardest problem — makes the LB itself not a single point of failure with a floating VIP that a standby claims in under two seconds, fenced by an etcd lease so it can't split-brain. The two halves connect through that shared health map and the gossip layer that propagates it. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: the box whose entire job is preventing outages must never itself cause one — so the data path stays available even when stale, and we refuse every global coordinator that would put a single point of failure back on the hot path."
